model_input:
Question: A man in his 50s presented to our dermatology department with an asymptomatic, arc-shaped, depressed skin groove on the left side of his forehead, frontal scalp, and vertex area. Localized linear hair loss and hair thinning on the left scalp area were observed. The patient reported that when the lesion had begun to appear, approximately 10 years earlier, he had received 2 doses of intralesional steroid via injection on a small bald patch on the vertex. Thereafter, the lesion gradually progressed to the frontal scalp and eventually made a linear furrow on the left side of his forehead.Physical examination revealed 2 long, curved-line, depressed, alopecic patches on the left side of the vertex and frontal scalp area extending to the left eyebrow (Figure, A and B). The lesions measured (length width) 7 2 cm and 6 1 cm, respectively. Dermoscopic examination of the alopecic area revealed several short vellus hairs, yellow dots, and honeycomb hyperpigmentation; however, broken hairs were not seen. The result of a hair pull test was negative. The patient denied any trauma history and had no history of connective tissue disease nor any evidence of systemic involvement, such as arthralgia, photosensitivity, or skin rashes. Skin biopsies were performed on the left side of the vertex and the forehead. Histopathologic findings of the 2 biopsy specimens were identical (Figure, C and D).A, Clinical image of annular arc from a longitudinally furrowed skin lesion on the left eyebrow and forehead to an alopecic lesion. B, Clinical image of the bandlike grooved alopecic lesion on the left side of the frontal scalp and vertex. C, Scanning view shows predominant lymphocytic infiltrates in perifollicular and periappendageal regions of the dermis and lobular panniculitis (hematoxylin-eosin staining). D, High-power view shows inflammatory infiltrates between adipocytes with hyaline fat necrosis in the subcutis (hematoxylin-eosin staining).Linear or annular lupus erythematosus panniculitis of the scalp What Is Your Diagnosis?
Options: A:Linear atrophoderma of Moulin B:Linear discoid lupus erythematosus C:Linear or annular lupus erythematosus panniculitis of the scalp D:Localized lipodystrophy
 Which option is the correct answer to the question? ALWAYS provide the option index of the final answer AT THE END with TRIPLE BACKTICKS among A, B, C, D, EXACTLY like this: The answer is ```X```. DO NOT add any other text or variations.
opc: Linear or annular lupus erythematosus panniculitis of the scalp
diagnosis: C. Linear or annular lupus erythematosus panniculitis of the scalp
answer: Linear or annular lupus erythematosus panniculitis of the scalp
explanation: Histopathologic examination revealed prominent lymphocytic infiltration around the hair follicles and appendages in the dermis, and vacuolar degeneration in the basal layer of the hair follicles without epidermal involvement. In the subcutaneous tissue layer, a lobular lymphocytic infiltrate was present with associated hyaline fat necrosis (Figure, C and D). Abundant mucin deposition in the deep dermis and subcutaneous fat layer were highlighted by Alcian blue staining. Direct immunofluorescence results were positive, with granular deposition of immunoglobulin (Ig) G, IgA, IgM, C3, C1q, and fibrinogen in the dermal blood vessels. All laboratory test findings were normal, including negativity for antinuclear antibody and antidouble-stranded DNA antibody. A diagnosis of lupus erythematosus panniculitis (LEP), specifically linear or annular lupus erythematosus panniculitis of the scalp (LALPS), was made. Slight improvement of the depressed skin lesion was observed with oral hydroxychloroquine (400 mg/d) and topical corticosteroid for 3 months.A unique clinical subtype of LEP, LALPS involves the deep dermis and subcutaneous fat layer of the skin.1,2 Classically, LEP presents as deep subcutaneous nodules or plaques, sometimes occurring in crops.1,2 Typically, it has predilection for the face and proximal extremities.1 When LEP occurs on the scalp along the Blaschko lines, it is termed LALPS,2 a condition first reported by Nagai and colleagues3 in 2003 and renamed by Mitxelena and colleagues in 2013.4Because LALPS is known to have a low rate of progression to systemic disease and spares the upper segment of hair follicles, it has a good prognosis.5 Compared with classic LEP, LALPS has a discriminative feature: its nonscarring nature and distribution follows the Blaschko lines of the scalp.2 Nevertheless, the extent of inflammation varies and may affect the scarring process of alopecia and the overall prognosis. When the inflammation is confined to the subcutaneous layer, the upper segments of the hair follicles and the stem cells in the hair bulge area are relatively well preserved; however, the contrary may lead to subsequent scarring alopecia.2,6 Therefore, early diagnosis and proper management are crucial. The predilection of LALPS for the Blaschko lines is not fully understood; however, it has been hypothesized that during early embryogenesis, aberrant genetically programmed cloning of cells may give rise to cells along the Blaschko lines that are highly susceptible to disease.7This case report describes an interesting presentation of LALPS, a condition that may be misdiagnosed as linear atrophoderma of Moulin, linear discoid lupus erythematosus, localized lipodystrophy, or en coup de sabre. Linear atrophoderma of Moulin is a rare clinical entity that follows the Blaschko lines. Its predilection for trunk or extremities and its histopathologic features, including basal hyperpigmentation and mild perivascular lymphocytic infiltrations preserving skin appendages, are helpful for differential diagnosis.8 Linear discoid lupus erythematosus is usually accompanied by epidermal changes such as scleroatrophy, hyperpigmentation, and follicular hyperkeratosis.9 Accordingly, compared with LALPS on histologic examination, LDLE more commonly involves the epidermal layer and has less involvement of the subcutaneous layer. Localized lipodystrophy may appear as an annular or semicircular subtype; however, its histopathologic features are generally noninflammatory.10 In en coup de sabre, linear morphea on the frontoparietal scalp and forehead appears as a paramedian band resembling a  sword s stroke. 7 Morphea exhibits histopathologic features of dermal sclerosis with eosinophilic swollen collagen fibers.Previous reports of LALPS cases have shown that treatment with hydroxychloroquine, corticosteroids (systemic, intralesional, or topical), methotrexate, mycophenolate mofetil, cyclophosphamide, dapsone, thalidomide, gold therapy, or topical minoxidil has been effective.5,6,9 Although the prognosis of LALPS is favorable, diagnostic delay and progression to scarring alopecia may leave lipoatrophic scars and cosmetic problems. Although little information is available on LALPS, it should be considered as a differential diagnosis of linear and annular alopecia or furrowed skin lesion on the scalp.
Question: A man in his 50s presented to our dermatology department with an asymptomatic, arc-shaped, depressed skin groove on the left side of his forehead, frontal scalp, and vertex area. Localized linear hair loss and hair thinning on the left scalp area were observed. The patient reported that when the lesion had begun to appear, approximately 10 years earlier, he had received 2 doses of intralesional steroid via injection on a small bald patch on the vertex. Thereafter, the lesion gradually progressed to the frontal scalp and eventually made a linear furrow on the left side of his forehead.Physical examination revealed 2 long, curved-line, depressed, alopecic patches on the left side of the vertex and frontal scalp area extending to the left eyebrow (Figure, A and B). The lesions measured (length width) 7 2 cm and 6 1 cm, respectively. Dermoscopic examination of the alopecic area revealed several short vellus hairs, yellow dots, and honeycomb hyperpigmentation; however, broken hairs were not seen. The result of a hair pull test was negative. The patient denied any trauma history and had no history of connective tissue disease nor any evidence of systemic involvement, such as arthralgia, photosensitivity, or skin rashes. Skin biopsies were performed on the left side of the vertex and the forehead. Histopathologic findings of the 2 biopsy specimens were identical (Figure, C and D).A, Clinical image of annular arc from a longitudinally furrowed skin lesion on the left eyebrow and forehead to an alopecic lesion. B, Clinical image of the bandlike grooved alopecic lesion on the left side of the frontal scalp and vertex. C, Scanning view shows predominant lymphocytic infiltrates in perifollicular and periappendageal regions of the dermis and lobular panniculitis (hematoxylin-eosin staining). D, High-power view shows inflammatory infiltrates between adipocytes with hyaline fat necrosis in the subcutis (hematoxylin-eosin staining).Linear or annular lupus erythematosus panniculitis of the scalp What Is Your Diagnosis?
raw_response:
Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this medical case. Let me go through the information step by step.

The patient is a man in his 50s who presented with an asymptomatic, arc-shaped, depressed skin groove on his left forehead, scalp, and vertex area. He also has localized linear hair loss and thinning on the left scalp. About 10 years ago, he received two doses of intralesional steroids for a small bald patch at the vertex. After that, the lesion progressed to the frontal scalp and formed a linear furrow on the left side of his forehead.

On physical exam, there are two long, curved, depressed, alopecic patches on the left vertex and frontal scalp, extending to the left eyebrow. The sizes are 7x2 cm and 6x1 cm. Dermoscopic exam shows short vellus hairs, yellow dots, and honeycomb hyperpigmentation without broken hairs. The hair pull test was negative, so it's not a sign of scarring or traction alopecia. He has no history of trauma, connective tissue disease, or systemic issues like arthralgia or skin rashes.

Skin biopsies were done on the vertex and forehead. The histopathology shows lymphocytic infiltrates in perifollicular and periappendageal regions, along with lobular panniculitis. In the high-power view, there are inflammatory infiltrates between adipocytes with hyaline fat necrosis in the subcutis.

The question is asking for the diagnosis among the options given.

Let me think about each option:

A: Linear atrophoderma of Moulin. I remember that this is a rare condition characterized by linear atrophy of the skin, often with a chevron-like configuration. It's usually seen in children and can be associated with systemic conditions, but the patient here is in his 50s and doesn't have systemic issues. Also, the histopathology doesn't mention atrophy, more so inflammation and panniculitis.

B: Linear discoid lupus erythematosus. Discoid lupus is a form of lupus that affects the skin, often with scarring. It's characterized by erythematous, scaly plaques. The histopathology would show granulosa formation. In this case, the biopsy shows panniculitis and fat necrosis, which is more consistent with lupus panniculitis, not discoid.

C: Linear or annular lupus erythematosus panniculitis of the scalp. Lupus panniculitis typically presents as subcutaneous lesions, often with a linear or annular arrangement. It's associated with systemic lupus erythematosus, but in this case, the patient doesn't have systemic symptoms. However, the histopathology here shows lobular panniculitis and hyaline fat necrosis, which aligns with lupus panniculitis. The fact that it's on the scalp and the linear/annular pattern fits this diagnosis.

D: Localized lipodystrophy. This condition causes localized loss of fat, leading to lipomas or other subcutaneous changes. The histopathology here doesn't show lipodystrophy; instead, it shows inflammation and panniculitis, so this is unlikely.

Putting it all together, the patient's history of prior steroid injections, the clinical presentation of linear alopecia with panniculitis on the scalp, and the histopathology pointing towards lupus panniculitis make option C the most likely diagnosis.
</think>

The patient's presentation, history, and histopathology are consistent with linear or annular lupus erythematosus panniculitis of the scalp.

The answer is ```C```
